texts.shortText: Distinctive species with an almost perfectly round, conical shell rising to a central sharp point. Last whorl greatly enlarged and flattened with an internal flat shell that protects the visceral organs. Margin of shelf distinctly convex and lobed. Exterior of shell white, usually smooth but may be sculpted with small knobs.
Shell up to about 15 mm in length.

Endemic to South Africa.

Local/Regional: False Bay to Eastern Cape, low intertidal to 90 m.
Usually found singly or in small groups on other shells or the undersides of boulders or in other sheltered habitats.
Filter-feeder, removing small particles from the surrounding sea water by passing this through the enlarged, sieve-like gills.

Protandrous hermaphrodite, changing from male to female as growth proceeds. Females lay >10 egg capsules of 2-3 mm in size that are attached to the substrate and sheltered under females’ shells. These each contain 20-60 embryonal larvae which hatch into crawling young without a free-swimming larval stage.

The shell shape and its predominantly smooth surface are distinctive. Progressively replaced by the larger (up to 45 mm) Trochita helicoidea Sowerby, 1883 from Algoa Bay eastwards. That species has a similar shell shape, but has radial ribs on outer surface that are tinged with orange-brown. Two more species occur in the same region, C. barnardi Kilburn, 1980 and C. aurita (Reeve, 1859), but these are potentially rare and hardly depicted in regional field guides. And image of C. barnardi is available in Steyn & Lussi (1998).

Branch, G, Griffiths, CL, Branch, M & Beckley, L (2016). Two Oceans: A Guide to the Marine Life of Southern Africa. (Fifth edition) Struik Nature, Cape Town. (p. 172).

Kilburn, R & Rippey, E (1982). Sea Shells of Southern Africa. Macmillan South Africa, Johannesburg (p. 57 ).

Wyatt, HV (1961). The reproduction, growth and distribution of Calyptraea chinensis (L.). The Journal of Animal Ecology, 30, 283-302.
